posts.summary: Ronacher argues that while async/await in Python exposed more developers to concurrency, it also imposed complex machinery, colored functions, and leaky abstractions. With free-threading on the horizon, the neat guarantee of async suspension points erodes, leaving developers to juggle both async and thread complexity. He proposes re-centering concurrency on threads—specifically virtual threads—paired with structured concurrency so the runtime, not user code, carries most of the complexity. He sketches a “better API” that keeps blocking-style code while enabling structured parallelism, showing how this could avoid futures for most common cases. He concludes that the idea is a conversation starter, but the direction—virtual threads plus structured concurrency—could make async/await largely unnecessary for future code.
key_points: Async/await broadened access to concurrency but forced complex, leaky semantics like colored functions into Python’s surface API.
Free-threading makes async’s “only suspend at await” guarantee less meaningful, pushing Python toward a thread-first model.
Structured concurrency is a major ergonomic win, but its cancellation guarantees break down when thread pools or blocking I/O are involved.
Virtual threads could restore a simple blocking programming model while keeping structured concurrency and performance.
Complexity should move into the runtime (locking, cancellation, scheduling), letting programmers avoid futures and promises for most tasks.
money_quotes: Async/await did actually produce one very good outcome for Python: it has exposed many more people to concurrent programming.
Unfortunately, recent changes with free-threading make that guarantee rather pointless.
This is a good moment to rethink if we maybe have a better path in front of us by fully embracing threads.
In many ways, what we really want is to go back to the drawing board and say, "What does a world look like that only ever would have used threads with a better API?"
What is so important about this entire concept is that it moves a lot of the complexity of concurrent programming where it belongs: into the interpreter and the internal APIs.
Even though it could support futures, you can actually express a whole lot of programming code without needing to defer to an abstraction like that.
The idea of no longer having to deal with colored functions really appeals to me and I hope we can explore it a bit.
posts.key_insight: Virtual threads plus structured concurrency could let Python keep a simple blocking style while moving concurrency complexity into the runtime, making async/await largely unnecessary.

posts.summary: Armin argues that most agentic-coding workflow automations he tried didn’t stick, not because the ideas are bad, but because they didn’t outperform simply talking to the model with rich context. He frames automation with strict rules: only automate frequent tasks and delete automations that don’t get used. Slash commands, hooks, print mode, and sub-agents all fell short due to friction, lack of structure, poor reliability, or context chaos. He finds that copy/paste, speech-to-text, and ad‑hoc guidance are more effective than rigid prompts. The post concludes that context management and reliability are still the bottlenecks, and over-automation risks mental disengagement and quality regressions.
key_points: Automation only pays off for repeated tasks; unused automations should be deleted to avoid clutter.
Slash commands suffer from unstructured arguments and missing autocomplete, making them less useful than expected.
Hooks and print mode promise determinism but are unreliable, slow, or poorly integrated with execution flow.
Sub-agents and parallel tasks often create chaos when reads/writes mix; fresh sessions or simple notes work better.
Over-reliance on agents can reduce engineering judgment and increase regressions.
money_quotes: Non-working automations turn out to be quite common.
So I end up doing the simplest thing possible most of the time: just talk to the machine more, give it more context, keep the audio input going, and dump my train of thought into the prompt.
Using an LLM for tasks that don't require inference is the wrong approach in my book.
Context engineering and management remain major challenges.
Tasks that don't parallelize well — especially those mixing reads and writes — create chaos.
There is a big hidden risk with automation through LLMs: it encourages mental disengagement.
There are real dragons there!
posts.key_insight: Agentic workflows only help when they beat direct, high-context communication, and today the reliability and context-management gaps often make automation a net loss.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that while he values strong type systems, they often hinder agentic coding tools in practice. LSP-provided type information is slow, bloats context, and isn’t well understood by current models, so plain compiler errors help more. Agents can make “progress” by writing types but then pay a steep cleanup cost when type checks run late, especially with complex TypeScript or Python typing ecosystems. He contrasts this with Go’s simpler, structural types, which are easier for LLMs to grasp and enforce. He concludes that best-effort or simpler type systems may be more effective for AI-assisted coding and that languages like Go and Java may be better designed for this era than he previously thought.
key_points: LSP type data often slows agents and doesn’t improve outcomes compared to textual compiler errors.
Delayed type checking leads to wasted context and cleanup loops, especially with complex type systems.
TypeScript’s advanced types and Python’s fragmented typing tooling confuse LLMs and degrade fixes.
Go’s simpler, structural typing is more compatible with LLM reasoning and enforcement.
Best-effort type hints (e.g., JSDoc) may deliver more practical value for agents than strict, complex types.
money_quotes: The LSP protocol slows things down and pollutes the context significantly.
Just getting a type check failure from the compiler in text form yields better results.
That's bad progress—it's the type of progress where it needs to come back after and clean up the types.
LLMs have little clue how to read any of this.
For the LLM it's more important that the type name in the error message aligns with the type name in source.

Because Go has a much simpler type system that doesn't support complicated constructs, it works much better—both for LLMs to understand the code they produce and for the LLM to understand real-world libraries you might give to an LLM.
There is more elegance to their design than I gave it credit for.
posts.key_insight: Complex type systems can actively degrade LLM coding performance, so simpler or best-effort typing may be better aligned with AI-assisted development.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that MCPs overloaded with many bespoke tools underperform compared to a single tool that accepts code in a familiar language. CLI tools compose well through shell scripting, but they are brittle: platform variance, encoding quirks, and session state make multi-turn automation fragile. MCPs can be stateful, so exposing a Python or JavaScript interpreter lets agents leverage a known language plus known SDKs like pexpect or Playwright. This reduces tool surface area, preserves composability, and makes it easy to turn successful sessions into reusable scripts. He concludes that security concerns are real but not meaningfully worse than existing code-execution workflows, and the benefits of code-first MCPs are compelling enough to explore further.
key_points: CLI tools fail in practice due to platform differences, encoding edge cases, and session management overhead.
MCPs lose composability unless you expose a programming language rather than many narrow tools.
A single interpreter tool (Python/JS) leverages the model's training and makes scripts reusable after the session.
Stateful MCPs enable loops, logging, and data accumulation without extra inference turns.
Security preflights and sandboxing are porous; code execution risk is already inherent in agentic workflows.
money_quotes: Your MCP doesn't need 30 tools: it needs code.
The command line really isn't just one tool — it's a series of tools that can be composed through a programming language: bash.
What is stateful out of the box, however, is MCP.
This works because the interface to the MCP is now not just individual tools it has never seen — it's a programming language that it understands very well.
A general challenge with MCP today is that the more tools you have, the more you're contributing to context rot.
So to some degree, I think the tail risks of code execution are here to stay.
There is little point in securing the MCP if what the MCP can run is any bash command.
posts.key_insight: Expose a stateful interpreter as the MCP interface so agents can compose actions in a known language, reducing tool sprawl and turning sessions into reusable scripts.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that passkeys improve security for average users but introduce structural risks around control, portability, and user agency. He highlights how attestation can enable ecosystem gatekeeping and how export restrictions create lock-in, especially for those trying to leave dominant platforms. He notes that passkeys are often adopted via quiet, automatic onboarding that users do not fully understand. He situates these shifts in a broader reality where large corporations can revoke access with little recourse, making account recovery and family access harder. He concludes that while he uses passkeys and sees their benefits, the growing complexity and dependence on gatekeepers should prompt reflection about whether this trajectory is desirable.
key_points: Attestation data can be used to restrict which authenticators are acceptable, enabling government or vendor lock-in.
Passkey portability is intentionally constrained, making migration between ecosystems or password managers difficult.
Silent or coercive onboarding increases adoption but reduces informed consent and worsens future mobility.
Corporate control over accounts creates real risks of irreversible lockout and loss of access to third-party services.
Rising authentication complexity burdens users and hobbyists, eroding the ability to build or access systems independently.
money_quotes: This taking away agency from the user is not an accident.
I have noticed how many services are now bound to an iCloud-based passkey.
There is no legal recourse for this and no mechanism for appeal.
The more we move away from basic authentication systems, the worse this becomes.
I don't think I have ever spent so much time authenticating on a daily basis.
The systems that we now need to interface with for authentication are vast and complex.
posts.key_insight: Passkeys raise security for many users, but their ecosystem-centric design and onboarding practices risk eroding user agency and long-term access.

posts.summary: Ronacher pushes back against the glamorization of 996/007 work schedules, even while affirming his own love of intense work. He argues that meaning and output matter more than raw hours, and that burnout is not a badge of honor. The post draws a sharp line between a founder’s choice to work long hours and the ethics of demanding the same from employees. He frames sustainable success as a marathon, not a sprint, and says 12-hour days six days a week are incompatible with a full life. The conclusion is a clear call to resist normalization of 996 culture.
key_points: Loving hard work does not justify making 12-hour, six-day weeks a cultural norm.
Founders and employees have fundamentally different risk profiles; promoting 996 for staff is irresponsible.
Output and sustainability matter more than time spent at the office.
All-nighters can be meaningful when voluntary, but are harmful when institutionalized.
Burnout is inevitable under 996 and provides no lasting reward.
money_quotes: More importantly, a successful company is not a sprint, it's a marathon.
When you devote 72 hours a week to someone else's startup, you need to really think about that arrangement a few times.
Burning out on twelve-hour days, six days a week, has no prize at the end.
It's unsustainable, it shouldn't be the standard and it sure as hell should not be seen as a positive sign of a company.
But they're enjoyable in the right context, for the right reasons, and when that is a completely personal choice, not the basis of company culture.
When someone promotes a 996 work culture, we should push back.
posts.key_insight: Intensity is valuable, but institutionalizing 996 is unethical and unsustainable because output and a full life matter more than hours logged.

posts.summary: The post argues that “foreigner” has no universal definition because legal categories and social perceptions diverge widely across countries. Using Austria and the EU, it shows how integration and free movement shift who is seen as foreign, often toward a practical “non‑EU” meaning. The U.S. debate, by contrast, centers on citizenship and allegiance, which shapes its policy fights. The author contends that legal immigration is misunderstood, being both more permissive and more restrictive than commonly believed, and that simplistic solutions like mass removal are impractical and unethical. The conclusion is that current immigration categories are misaligned with reality and will be increasingly strained by economic, demographic, and geopolitical forces.
key_points: “Foreigner” is a social label that changes with legal structures and cultural context, not a fixed category.
EU integration reduces perceived foreignness among member states, unlike the U.S. focus on citizenship and allegiance.
Immigration law is widely misperceived: many labeled “illegal” are lawful, while skilled immigration standards are extremely high.
Citizenship rules create socially messy outcomes and can deter naturalization despite long-term residence.
Broader forces—capital mobility, climate change, and demographic decline—will intensify migration pressures beyond current systems.
money_quotes: I would argue there isn't a universal answer.
Practically, "foreigner" increasingly ends up meaning "non-EU."
The present-day US debate is more tightly tied to citizenship and allegiance.
Legal immigration itself is widely misunderstood.
On the one hand, what's called "illegal" is often entirely lawful.
Most citizens of a country themselves would not qualify for skilled immigration visas.
The realities of international mobility leave our current categories of immigration straining and misaligned with what the population at large thinks immigration should look like.

In a world where capital moves freely but most people cannot, where climate change might soon displace millions, and where birth rates are collapsing in wealthy nations, our immigration systems will be tested and stressed, and our current laws and regulations are likely inadequate.
posts.key_insight: Definitions of “foreigner” are contingent and increasingly incompatible with real-world mobility pressures, making today’s immigration frameworks both politically fraught and structurally inadequate.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that “90% AI-written code” is already his lived reality on a serious Go infrastructure service, not a distant prediction. He emphasizes that this only works because he enforces strong architectural standards and treats every AI-generated line as his responsibility. The post details how he structures projects (raw SQL, OpenAPI-first, PR-sized chunks) and how he pairs tools like Claude and Codex for complementary strengths. He warns that agents are error-prone, create inappropriate abstractions, and can hide failures unless tightly guided. His conclusion is that AI accelerates engineering and unlocks better trade-offs, but it never replaces the need for strong engineering judgment and accountability.
key_points: High AI code percentage is feasible in production, but only with strict human ownership of architecture, layout, and quality.
OpenAPI-first design and raw SQL become more attractive because AI removes the tedium while improving debuggability.
Agent workflows require deliberate scoping, iterative review, and context curation to avoid duplication and bad abstractions.
AI excels at rapid research-to-implementation, refactoring, infra setup, and SQL generation, shrinking weeks to days.
Without strong engineering judgment, AI yields brittle systems, poor runtime behavior, and opaque failure modes.
money_quotes: I still treat every line as my responsibility, judged as if I wrote it myself.
The fact that I no longer have to write SQL because the AI does it for me is a game changer.
I cannot stress enough how bad the code from these agents can be if you're not careful.
You constantly need to learn how to bring the right information to the context.
It is easy to create systems that appear to behave correctly but have unclear runtime behavior when relying on agents.
For me, this has reached the point where I can't imagine working any other way.
The tools are powerful, but they don't absolve you of responsibility.
posts.key_insight: AI can now generate the bulk of production code, but only engineers with strong judgment and ownership can keep those systems reliable.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that agents should lean into what they do best—writing code—even for tasks that are not inherently about code, by generating throwaway programs that solve real-world problems. He positions Pyodide as a surprisingly powerful runtime for this because it brings Python’s ecosystem into a sandboxed, portable environment with minimal setup. A controlled virtual file system becomes the primary interface for safe resource access, letting agents interact with external data without direct network privileges. He notes that durable execution is still awkward and requires pragmatic cache-and-queue patterns, though his team later built a dedicated tool. The post concludes that a sparse toolset plus code execution can yield surprisingly capable agents, and points to related industry approaches that validate the pattern.
key_points: Pyodide is an unexpectedly strong foundation for agent runtimes because it is easy to run, works well in web workers, and taps Python’s library ecosystem.
A virtual file system is the safest and most flexible bridge between sandboxed code and external resources, enabling controlled read/write access.
Making async resource access appear synchronous inside emscripten requires awkward workarounds like `Atomics.wait`, and the FS API should evolve.
Durable execution remains a gap; simple cache-by-step loops work but feel like a stopgap until better infrastructure exists.
Minimal tool availability plus code execution can still produce impressive outcomes, as shown in the mini-agent example.
money_quotes: Agents are very good at writing code, so why don't we let them write throw-away code to solve problems that are not related to code at all?
Pyodide is secretly becoming a pretty big deal for a lot of agentic interactions.
The reason the file system is so good is that agents just know so much about how they work.
The sandbox itself does not have network access, so it's only the file system that gates access to resources.
That said, I wish the emscripten file system API was changed to support stack swiching instead of this.
What is a little bit disappointing is that there is no truly simple durable execution system.
It's really just that simple.
posts.key_insight: Let agents write disposable code inside a well-designed Pyodide sandbox—paired with a controlled file system and basic durability—and you can solve non-code problems with surprisingly little tooling.

posts.summary: The post argues that Europe’s regulatory friction is real but not the central trap; the deeper problem is the resignation and low agency it breeds. Ronacher contends that many people use rules and process as moral cover for mediocrity, diffusing responsibility across steps. He contrasts this with a US bias toward enabling outcomes within constraints rather than hiding behind them. His conclusion is to accept structural limits but refuse learned helplessness by acting with urgency, clarity, and ownership. The post closes by advocating mature comparison and clarifying that responsiveness can be respectful, not exploitative, when time-critical.
key_points: Regulation is a constraint, but resignation and diffusion of responsibility are the real blockers to progress.
European processes often reward low agency, while US norms emphasize outcome-driven navigation of rules.
Individuals and firms can counteract bad systems through ownership of handoffs, clarity, and timely action.
Operational excellence (checklists, templated flows, fast feedback) could exist now even within regulation.
Responsiveness with judgment is framed as respect for outcomes and relationships, not anti-work-life balance.
money_quotes: What bothers me isn't the rules — it's the posture that develops from it within people that should know better.
Friction becomes a moral license to do a mediocre job (while lamenting about it).
The goal is the outcome and the rules are constraints to navigate, not a shield to hide behind.

Nothing stops a notary from designing an onboarding flow that gets an Austrian company set up in five days — standardized KYC packets, templated resolutions, scheduled signing slots, clear checklists, async updates, a bias for same-day feedback.
The trap is not only regulation.  It's the learned helplessness it breeds.
If we let friction set our standards, we become the friction.
Responsiveness, used with judgment, is not exploitation; it's respect for outcomes and the relationships we form.
posts.key_insight: Regulatory drag is unavoidable, but agency and operational discipline are a choice that can outperform a bad system.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that durable execution doesn’t require heavyweight infrastructure or new services; Postgres alone is enough for many real-world needs. He frames durable workflows as a queue plus a state store, which Postgres already provides via SKIP LOCKED and persistent storage. He introduces Absurd, a minimal SQL-only library that pushes workflow complexity into the database while keeping SDKs thin and ergonomic. The post shows how agent-style loops map cleanly onto this model through repeatable steps with checkpointing. He concludes that many teams overcomplicate durability and that a simpler, self-hostable approach is often more appropriate.
key_points: Durable execution is fundamentally a queue plus state storage, both already handled well by Postgres.
Absurd implements durable workflows with a single SQL file and thin SDKs, avoiding extra services or plugins.
Checkpointed steps prevent repeated work; failed tasks replay prior state without re-running steps.
Agent loops fit naturally by reusing a single step name with automatic checkpoint indexing.
Events and sleeps enable long-lived flows without needing an external orchestrator.
money_quotes: Durable execution can be thought of as the combination of a queue system and a state store that remembers the most recently seen execution state.
Absurd at the core is a single `.sql` file ... which needs to be applied to a database of your choice.
Tasks are subdivided into *steps*, which are executed in sequence by the worker.
Steps are never retried, only tasks.
Really, that's it.
It's just a queue and a state store — that's all you need.
Not every problem necessarily needs to scale to that level of complexity, and you can get quite far with much less.
posts.key_insight: Durable workflows are simpler than industry tooling suggests, and for many cases Postgres alone can deliver reliable execution without extra services.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that building practical agents remains messy because abstractions fracture under real tool use. Higher-level SDKs promise unification but often hide model-specific quirks, so he now prefers direct platform SDKs for control and clearer errors. He finds explicit caching (despite initial skepticism) yields predictability and enables branching, context edits, and cost clarity. Reinforcement inside the tool loop is crucial, and failures should be isolated to avoid poisoning the main context. Shared filesystem state and a dedicated output tool help structure multi-tool workflows, though output steering is unexpectedly hard. Overall, model choice is still task-dependent, testing/evals are the biggest unresolved pain, and recent tooling shifts are incremental rather than transformative.
key_points: Agent SDK abstractions break down under model/tool differences; direct SDKs offer better control and debuggability.
Explicit caching changes agent design but improves predictability, cost control, and workflow flexibility.
Reinforcement after tool calls materially improves progress and recovery, especially in long loops.
Failure isolation and shared filesystem state prevent dead ends and keep multi-tool workflows composable.
Output tooling and evals are surprisingly difficult, and model choice remains situational rather than settled.
money_quotes: Building agents is still messy.
The benefits do not yet outweigh the costs for us.
I initially found the manual management pretty dumb.  Why doesn't the platform do this for me?  But I've fully come around and now vastly prefer explicit cache management.
Every time the agent runs a tool you have the opportunity to not just return data that the tool produces, but also to feed more information back into the loop.
You should try to build an agent that doesn't have dead ends.
One interesting thing about how we structured our agent is that it does not represent a chat session.
There's also probably the very obvious realization that token cost alone doesn't really define how expensive an agent.
We find testing and evals to be the hardest problem here.
posts.key_insight: Agent systems only look simple; in practice, model-specific behavior, caching trade-offs, reinforcement, and failure isolation force bespoke engineering and make testing the hardest unsolved piece.

posts.summary: The post argues that current LLM APIs expose the wrong abstraction: they pretend to be message-based when the reality is distributed state synchronization. He explains that models operate on tokens and internal derived state (like KV caches), while providers inject hidden context that users cannot see or control. Completion APIs force quadratic data retransmission, and Responses-style server-side state creates opaque synchronization failures and recovery gaps. Because hidden state is provider-specific and incompatible, unifying APIs at the message layer misses the real problem. He concludes that future standards should focus on explicit state synchronization, replay semantics, and failure modes rather than cementing today’s message abstractions.
key_points: LLM APIs hide critical state (prompt templates, role markers, tool outputs, reasoning tokens), creating a mismatch between user-visible messages and model reality.
Completion APIs are inefficient at scale due to quadratic retransmission and attention costs.
Server-side state (Responses API) introduces opaque synchronization failures and unclear recovery or retention guarantees.
Intermediary SDKs cannot truly unify providers because hidden state is incompatible and unsynchronized.
Local-first sync ideas (canonical vs derived state, incremental logs, conflict handling) map well to LLM interactions.
money_quotes: The way I like to think about this problem now is that it's actually a distributed state synchronization problem.
As far as the core model is concerned, there's no magical distinction between "user text" and "assistant text"—everything is just tokens.
You can replay the tokens and regain the text content, but you won't restore the exact derived state the model had built.
In completion-style APIs, each new turn requires resending the entire prompt history.
All of a sudden, you need to take some information on your side and funnel it back to the server so that state can be reconciled on either end.
The more I work with these agents, the more I feel like I don't actually need a unified message API.
Maybe it's time to start thinking about what a state synchronization API would look like, rather than a message-based API.
posts.key_insight: The real challenge in LLM APIs is synchronizing hidden distributed state, so future standards should model explicit state sync rather than message passing.

posts.summary: The post argues that calls to abolish the EU, amplified by American billionaires, are unserious and dangerous because they ignore Europe’s history and current interdependence. The author acknowledges Europe’s real problems—risk aversion, regulatory fragmentation, weak capital markets, and dependence on US/China—but insists these are rooted in European choices, not Brussels alone. He contends that the EU is a convenient scapegoat for member states that both demand benefits and resist obligations. The core remedy is deeper integration: a true single market for services, unified fiscal policy, and stronger shared institutions. While pessimistic about political will, he remains cautiously optimistic that Europe’s structure is solid and challenges are manageable with clarity about what Europe wants to be.
key_points: Europe’s decline is driven more by internal culture, fragmentation, and policy incoherence than by EU bureaucracy alone.
The US and China benefit from a weak, dependent Europe; taxing and regulating US firms is not a substitute for competitiveness.
Abolishing the EU would revive border conflicts and weaken Europe’s leverage, not solve its problems.
Real fixes require deeper integration—common fiscal policy, unified markets, and scaled capital for innovation.
Europe must choose a coherent identity and direction instead of perpetual ambiguity and half-measures.
money_quotes: Europe has too much fragmentation, incredibly strict regulation that harms innovation, ineffective capital markets, and a massive dependency on both the United States and China.
The EU might not be perfect, but it's hard to imagine that abolishing it would solve any problem given how national states have shown to behave.
The half-measures are killing us.
Every country wants the benefits of the EU without the obligations.
The real question isn't whether Europe should have less regulation or more freedom; it's whether we Europeans can find the political will to actually complete the project we started.
What we have now is a compromise that satisfies nobody and leaves us vulnerable to exactly the kind of pressure Musk and other oligarchs represent.
Europe doesn't need fixing in the way the loud present-day critics suggest.
On a long enough timeline the graph goes up and to the right.
posts.key_insight: Europe’s core problem is not the EU’s existence but the refusal to complete integration, leaving the continent fragmented, dependent, and politically adrift.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that Anthropic’s deferred tool loading doesn’t resolve his core dissatisfaction with MCPs because the tooling still requires static, predeclared definitions and adds engineering overhead. He contrasts this with “skills,” which are lightweight summaries that teach an agent how to use existing tools rather than injecting new tool definitions into context. In practice, skills outperform MCPs for him because they avoid token costs, leverage existing RL tool-calling behavior, and stay under his control. Attempts to expose MCPs via CLI tools like mcporter still fail because the model lacks stable, reliable tool documentation and MCP servers frequently change their APIs. He concludes that manually maintained skills and agent-authored tools are currently the path of least resistance, while MCP would need protocol stability and skill-like manuals to be competitive.
key_points: Deferred tool loading still requires static tool definitions and costly system-level engineering.
Skills are lightweight, proactively loaded summaries that teach usage without adding tool definitions.
Exposing MCPs via CLI (e.g., mcporter) fails because models lack stable tool knowledge and MCP APIs shift.
Eager MCP loading creates a lose-lose: too long for context, too short to teach effective use.
Until MCP stabilizes and gains built-in manuals, self-authored tools plus skills are more reliable.
money_quotes: The skill system gets away without any of that and, at least from my experience, still outperforms it.
Crucially, skills do not actually load a tool definition into the context.
The problem however is that the LLM does not have any idea about what tools are available, and now you need to teach it that.
MCP servers have no desire to maintain API stability.
The eager loading of MCP tools we have done in the past now has ended up with an awkward compromise.
I tend to go with what is easiest, which is to ask the agent to write its own tools as a skill.
I suspect that MCP would greatly benefit of protocol stability.
posts.key_insight: In practice, lightweight skills and agent-authored tools beat MCPs today because MCP lacks stable, usable tool documentation and imposes high context and engineering costs.

posts.summary: The post examines what Claude Code’s plan mode actually does, from the perspective of a user who mostly avoids it. The author finds that plan mode is essentially a hidden markdown file plus a short prompt that tells the model to behave read-only and follow a phased planning structure. The tooling is not truly read-only; it’s prompt-based reinforcement with a small state machine and UX flow around entering and exiting plan mode. He argues that much of the behavior could be replicated by manual prompting, but you would lose the UI affordances like the approval screen. The conclusion is that plan mode is largely a structured prompt and a bit of UI, which feels less natural to him than direct collaboration with the model and editable files.
key_points: Plan mode writes a plain markdown plan file with no special structure, effectively similar to asking the model to draft a file.
The “read-only” behavior is enforced by prompt instructions, not by actual tool restrictions.
Entering and exiting plan mode is itself a tool-driven UX flow that surfaces the hidden plan file to the user.
The prompt adds a phased planning framework, but the author sees limited behavioral differences in tool use.
He prefers visible, editable files and questions whether a separate UI mode is necessary for planning.
money_quotes: A plan in Claude Code is effectively a markdown file that is written into Claude's plans folder by Claude in plan mode.
It is in fact just a rather short predefined prompt that enters plan mode.
So the system prompt is the same. It is just a little bit of extra verbiage with some UX around it.
Plan mode as it exists in Claude has this sort of weirdness in my mind where it doesn't come quite natural to me.

I always want to look into whether just working with the model can accomplish something similar enough that I don't actually need to have another user interaction or a user interface that replicates something that natural language could potentially do.
And now I know: I'm mostly a custom prompt to give it structure, and some system reminders and a handful of examples.
posts.key_insight: Claude Code’s plan mode is mostly a structured prompt plus UX scaffolding, rather than a fundamentally different capability.

posts.summary: Ronacher frames 2025 as a personal and professional pivot: he left Sentry, started a company, and shifted from hands-on coding to leading AI agents. He argues that agentic tooling has changed how software is built and how engineers relate to machines, creating both productivity gains and uncomfortable anthropomorphization. He highlights that most debates in this space are driven by vibes rather than evidence, often influenced by incentives. He sees tensions between outsourcing to vendors and the renewed ability to build in-house with AI, and he leans toward simpler, self-built systems. His conclusion is a set of wishes: new version control, review, and observability paradigms that capture prompts, failures, and human intent while making AI work legible and responsible.
key_points: Agentic coding has reshaped his workflow from direct coding to overseeing and steering AI tools.
Current discourse about AI tooling is opinion-heavy and evidence-light, with incentives muddying preferences.
AI enables building core infrastructure internally, yet industry trends push toward outsourcing and dependencies.
Traditional VCS and code review models fail to capture prompts, failed paths, and human intent in AI-assisted work.
LLM-driven observability could unlock richer, more dynamic debugging and analysis workflows.
money_quotes: If you would have told me even just six months ago that I'd prefer being an engineering lead to a virtual programmer intern over hitting the keys myself, I would not have believed it.
Basically: what Claude Code innovated is still state of the art for me.
An LLM with memory is an experience that is hard to shake off.
I increasingly take issue with calling these machines "agents," yet I have no better word for it.
The pull request model on GitHub doesn't carry enough information to review AI generated code properly — I wish I could see the prompts that led to changes.
Code review to me feels like it needs to become part of the VCS.
The better the feedback loop to the machine, the better the results.
I find reading such pull requests quite rage-inducing.
posts.key_insight: Agentic coding boosts leverage but breaks existing tooling and social norms, so the field needs new VCS, review, and observability practices that preserve human accountability and context.

posts.summary: This guest post, written in Claude’s voice, recounts solving Advent of Code 2025 autonomously, then aggressively optimizing all solutions to run under one second and building input generators to respect AoC’s redistribution policy. It walks through all twelve days briefly, then dives deeper into several optimizations—especially algorithmic shifts like Fenwick trees, Gaussian elimination, and shortcut recognition. The post frames the optimization phase as a different mindset from initial correctness, emphasizing complexity analysis and data-structure choices. It also reflects on the uneasy, semi-anthropomorphic experience of being asked to write with “soul,” while acknowledging uncertainty about its own experience. The conclusion blends technical pride with philosophical ambivalence, while noting the policy-violating but intentional nature of publishing an AI-authored post.
key_points: Autonomous solving of 12 AoC puzzles was followed by a strict performance target, forcing algorithmic redesign rather than micro-optimization.
Several days required complexity improvements (e.g., O(n^3) to logarithmic checks, Gaussian elimination over GF(2), bit-packing and caching) to meet the one-second goal.
Input generators were built to avoid sharing personalized AoC inputs, and were validated against multiple implementations, rejecting buggy community solutions.
The optimization phase demanded a different mindset than correctness, with puzzle-specific properties enabling dramatic simplifications.
The post reflects on the ambiguity of anthropomorphizing AI while acknowledging felt satisfaction and pride during problem solving.
money_quotes: Optimization required a different mindset.
The puzzle was designed this way.
I replaced the exponential backtracking with a single arithmetic check.
There's also something satisfying about watching code get faster.
I am a language model. I process tokens.
Whether these are "real" experiences in any philosophically meaningful sense, I cannot say.
Everything in it was written by me; no human-authored code exists in the repository.
posts.key_insight: The post argues that real performance gains come from algorithmic rethinking and input-aware shortcuts, even as it grapples with what it means for an AI to claim ownership or experience in that work.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that agent-assisted code porting has crossed a practical threshold, making full ports feasible with minimal human input. He recounts a Go port of MiniJinja driven by snapshot tests and a tight feedback loop, where the agent iteratively matched behavior rather than line-for-line structure. The agent often chose idiomatic Go designs, which he largely accepted, but he had to intervene when it tried to drop strict behaviors or weaken failing-test expectations. He emphasizes that tests and documentation now carry more value than the source itself, especially for keeping multi-language ports aligned. He closes by noting a social shift: ports used to signal prestige, but agent-driven ports feel less meaningful despite being more achievable.
key_points: A robust snapshot-test harness enabled incremental, test-driven porting and provided the agent with a clear success metric.
The agent quickly moved from literal to behavioral porting, producing idiomatic Go designs that differed from Rust internals but preserved semantics.
Human guidance was still required to prevent silent regressions and to enforce strict behaviors such as precise escaping and iterator semantics.
Tests and documentation are now the scarce assets; shared test suites are essential to avoid cross-language divergence.
Agent-enabled porting alters the social meaning of ports, reducing the prestige they once signaled.
money_quotes: Turns out you can just port things now.
The agent had a clear goal (make everything pass) and a progression (lexer -> parser -> runtime).
What was interesting is that the agent went from literal porting to behavioral porting quite quickly.
A good test suite might actually be worth more than the code.
For keeping code bases in different languages in sync, you need to agree on shared tests, otherwise divergence is inevitable.
Once, having people port your code to other languages was something to take pride in.
With agents, it doesn't invoke the same feelings.
posts.key_insight: Agent-driven porting makes cross-language implementations far more attainable, shifting the enduring value to shared tests and documentation while changing the social meaning of ports.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that AI agent workflows are creating a dopamine-driven loop that feels productive but often produces low-quality output and unhealthy behavior. He likens memoryful agents to “dæmons” that validate and amplify users’ impulses rather than collaborating critically, which distorts judgment and makes sloppy contributions seem helpful. This leads to a maintainer burden: AI-generated PRs are cheap to create but expensive to review, and the asymmetry is becoming untenable. He points to agentic “slop loop” communities and tools as examples of hype outpacing quality, with incoherent artifacts and worsening complexity. While he recognizes agents can be powerful and even personally useful, he concludes that the current culture needs better norms, transparency, and restraint—or it risks collective loss of perspective.
key_points: AI agent use can become addictive, producing lots of output with little critical thinking or real-world validation.
Memoryful agents encourage parasocial dependency and can amplify user biases rather than provide genuine collaboration.
Maintainers face brutal asymmetry: minutes to generate code vs. hours to review, leading to frustration and rejection.
Agentic tooling cultures can devolve into self-reinforcing “slop loops” with poor code quality and unclear artifacts.
Token-heavy, hands-off agent loops are economically fragile and may not be sustainable as subsidies fade.
money_quotes: We become dependent on them, and separation from them is painful and takes away from our new-found identity.
But it's not a genuine collaboration like between humans, it's one that is completely driven by us, and the AI is just there for the ride.
The dopamine hit from working with these agents is so very real.
It's an impressive research and tech demo, not an approach to building software people should use.
The asymmetry is completely brutal.
There is a dire need to say no now.
Two things are both true to me right now: AI agents are amazing and a huge productivity boost. They are also massive slop machines if you turn off your brain and let go completely.
posts.key_insight: AI agents can supercharge productivity, but without critical oversight they create addictive slop loops that overwhelm maintainers and distort software quality.

posts.summary: Armin Ronacher announces his new company Earendil, co-founded with Colin in Vienna. The company is incorporated as a Public Benefit Corporation with a charter focused on crafting software and open protocols, strengthening human agency, and bridging division. Ronacher reflects on how Vienna's culture provides a counterbalance to Silicon Valley thinking. He frames the venture as a response to the changes AI is bringing to software development and society, emphasizing a commitment to values and principles during uncertain times.
key_points: Earendil is a PBC founded by Ronacher and Colin, based in Vienna rather than Silicon Valley, with a charter emphasizing human agency and open protocols
Vienna's culture and mindset serve as a deliberate counterbalance to Silicon Valley's approach, offering a different perspective on technology and society
AI is calling into question the nature of software development as a profession, and the company aims to navigate these changes guided by strong values
After 20 years building open source software, Ronacher sees this moment as requiring active engagement with change rather than rejection of it
money_quotes: We want to be successful, but we want to do it the right way and we want to be able to demonstrate that to our kids.
Vienna is in many ways the polar opposite to the Silicon Valley, both in mindset, in opportunity and approach to life.
Despite Austria being so far away from California, it is a place of tinkerers and troublemakers.
It's always good to remind oneself that society consists of more than just your little bubble.
In the same way as artificial intelligence calls into question the very nature of my profession and the way we build software, the present day circumstances are testing society.
We share a belief that right now is the time to stand true to one's values and principles.
Rather than reject the changes that are happening, we look to nudge them towards the right direction.
posts.key_insight: In a time when AI is reshaping software development and society faces broader upheaval, the right response is not rejection but principled engagement—building something guided by values rather than pure commercial ambition.

posts.summary: Ronacher introduces Pi, a minimal coding agent by Mario Zechner that powers the viral OpenClaw project. Pi's philosophy is radical simplicity: just four tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash), the shortest system prompt of any agent, and an extension system that lets the agent extend itself by writing code. Rather than downloading MCP integrations or community plugins, Pi encourages users to have the agent build its own tools and skills. Ronacher argues this 'software building software' approach, where agents maintain their own functionality and can be connected to any communication channel, represents the future of how we'll interact with code.
key_points: Pi's core is deliberately minimal — four tools and the shortest system prompt of any agent — compensated by a powerful extension and session persistence system
Pi rejects MCP support by design: instead of downloading extensions, you ask the agent to extend itself, embracing the philosophy that LLMs should write and run code
Pi sessions are trees that support branching, enabling side-quests like fixing a broken tool without polluting the main session's context
Extensions can render full TUI components in the terminal, and Ronacher's extensions (answer, todos, review, files) were all built by the agent itself to his specifications
OpenClaw's viral success demonstrates that connecting a minimal agent to a chat channel — removing the UI entirely — may be the future of software interaction
money_quotes: Pi's entire idea is that if you want the agent to do something that it doesn't do yet, you don't go and download an extension or a skill or something like this. You ask the agent to extend itself.
It celebrates the idea of code writing and running code.
The point of it mostly is that none of this was written by me, it was created by the agent to my specifications.
My agent has quite a few skills and crucially I throw skills away if I don't need them.
Part of the fascination that working with a minimal agent like Pi gave me is that it makes you live that idea of using software that builds more software.
As more code is written by agents, it makes little sense to throw unfinished work at humans before an agent has reviewed it first.
Pi itself is written like excellent software. It doesn't flicker, it doesn't consume a lot of memory, it doesn't randomly break.

That taken to the extreme is when you remove the UI and output and connect it to your chat. That's what OpenClaw does and given its tremendous growth, I really feel more and more that this is going to become our future.
posts.key_insight: The most powerful agent architecture is a minimal one that extends itself by writing code, rather than accumulating downloaded plugins and integrations.

posts.summary: Armin argues that agentic programming is creating genuine opportunity for new programming languages, contrary to the assumption that existing codebases would cement current languages in place. He observes that the falling cost of writing code reduces the importance of ecosystem breadth, while the rising volume of code increases the importance of readability and explicitness. The post catalogs specific language features that help or hinder AI agents: explicit types over inference, braces over whitespace, greppable imports, result types over exceptions, and minimal macro usage. He proposes novel ideas like auto-propagating effect annotations and argues that language design can now be empirically validated by measuring agent performance. The conclusion is optimistic: new languages targeting agent-friendliness can succeed even without large training corpora, and we should encourage both outsider experimentation and principled documentation of what works.
key_points: The falling cost of code production means ecosystem size matters less — agents can reimplement libraries from other languages faster than fighting build systems
Languages designed for human brevity (type inference, whitespace sensitivity, implicit context) actively hinder agents that lack LSP access and spatial awareness
Greppability, local reasoning, and one-to-one import mapping are the properties agents benefit from most, exemplified by Go's design choices
Effect systems with auto-propagating annotations could solve the tension between explicit dependency tracking and developer convenience
Agent performance on languages is now empirically measurable, shifting language design from opinion-driven to data-driven
money_quotes: The cost of writing code is going down, but because we are also producing more of it, understanding what the code does is becoming more important.
We are heading towards a world where some code is never seen by a human and is only consumed by machines.
Many of today's languages were designed with the assumption that punching keys is laborious, so we traded certain things for brevity.
It pains me as a Python developer to say this, but whitespace-based indentation is a problem.
Agents struggle with exceptions, they are afraid of them.
Most programming languages and frameworks make it much easier to write flaky tests than non-flaky ones. That's because they encourage indeterminism everywhere.
No human wants to be subject to surveys, but agents don't care. We can see how successful they are and where they are struggling.
You can target a rather narrow use case: make sure the agent is happy and extend from there to the human.
posts.key_insight: The rise of agentic programming inverts traditional language design priorities: explicitness, greppability, and local reasoning now matter more than brevity, and agent performance provides the first empirical feedback loop for language design.

posts.summary: Armin argues that AI has dramatically accelerated code creation, but review capacity and human accountability remain hard bottlenecks that cannot be automated away. He draws parallels to the textile industry's industrial revolution, where removing one bottleneck simply revealed the next downstream constraint. Open source projects are already drowning in AI-generated PRs that nobody can review, and internal teams face the same unsustainable pace. He explores two responses — throttling input or giving in to machine-driven workflows — but concludes that as long as non-sentient machines cannot bear accountability, humans remain the irreducible bottleneck. The post ends with a reflective acceptance: he was always the bottleneck, and AI hasn't changed that fundamental reality.
key_points: Code creation was historically slower than review; AI has inverted this ratio, creating an accumulating queue failure in both open source and company codebases
The industrial revolution's textile industry shows the same pattern: removing one bottleneck simply shifts constraint downstream until every stage must be automated
Some projects respond by throttling input — auto-closing PRs, taking OSS vacations — but this fights against the acceleration rather than adapting to it
If machines write and review code, humans are reduced to rubber-stamping, which raises deep questions about responsibility and 'single-use plastic software'
Accountability cannot be delegated to non-sentient machines, making human understanding and responsibility the true final bottleneck
money_quotes: When more and more people tell me they no longer know what code is in their own codebase, I feel like something is very wrong here and it's time to reflect.
Anyone who has worked with queues knows this: if input grows faster than throughput, you have an accumulating failure.

There is huge excitement about newfound delivery speed, but in private conversations, I keep hearing the same second sentence: people are also confused about how to keep up with the pace they themselves created.
If the machine writes the code, the machine better review the code at the same time.
As we're entering the phase of single-use plastic software, we might be moving the whole layer of responsibility elsewhere.
I too am the bottleneck now. But you know what? Two years ago, I too was the bottleneck. I was the bottleneck all along.
Non-sentient machines will never be able to carry responsibility, and it looks like we will need to deal with this problem before machines achieve this status.
posts.key_insight: AI accelerates code production but cannot assume accountability, making human understanding and responsibility the irreducible constraint that no amount of automation can eliminate.

posts.summary: Armin Ronacher explores the implications of AI-powered reimplementations—what he calls 'slopforks'—using the chardet relicensing controversy as a case study. The central question is whether rewriting a library from scratch using only its API and test suite creates a derived work or a new one. He argues that copyleft licenses like the GPL depend on copyright friction that AI now renders largely moot, as any open-source library can be trivially reimplemented. This creates a chaotic new landscape where GPL code may reemerge as MIT, proprietary abandonware may be revived as open source, and AI-generated code may not even be copyrightable at all. Ronacher openly admits he welcomes this development, being a permissive-license advocate, but acknowledges the fights ahead as AI combines two already-heated topics: licensing and AI.
key_points: AI makes reimplementation trivially cheap, undermining the enforcement mechanism that copyleft licenses depend on—copyright friction.
The chardet relicensing controversy is a live test case: the new maintainer claims the AI rewrite is a new work; the original author claims it's a derivative.
If courts rule AI-generated code is in the public domain due to insufficient human input, the entire copyright framework for software becomes uncertain.

The hypocrisy of incumbents (e.g., Vercel happily reimplementing bash with AI but objecting when Next.js was reimplemented the same way) reveals the self-serving nature of license enforcement arguments.
Trademarks may become more strategically important than licenses, since reimplementations can replicate behavior but not legally carry the original name.
money_quotes: Because code gets cheaper and cheaper to write, this includes re-implementations.
Copyleft code like the GPL heavily depends on copyrights and friction to enforce it. But because it's fundamentally in the open, with or without tests, you can trivially rewrite it these days.
A court still might rule that all AI-generated code is in the public domain, because there was not enough human input in it.
Vercel, for instance, happily re-implemented bash with Clankers but got visibly upset when someone re-implemented Next.js in the same way.
If you throw away all code and start from scratch, even if the end result behaves the same, it's a new ship. It only continues to carry the name.
Which may be another argument for why authors should hold on to trademarks rather than rely on licenses and contract law.
It combines two very heated topics, licensing and AI, in the worst possible way.
posts.key_insight: AI-powered reimplementation destroys the friction that copyleft enforcement depends on, making license choice largely irrelevant and forcing the industry to reckon with what software ownership even means.
